scene.name: No Man's Land, Where the War Dead Wait on Both Sides

exits.label: ◂ back into the lanes of Sigil

npcs.name: Ander
npcs.title: a soldier who killed a man he never knew
npcs.hail: *(A young man in the faded blue of a soldier sits with his back against a cairn, on his own side of the court, as far from the man in red across the way as the space allows — and the distance between them is the most eloquent thing in the room, two soldiers in death keeping the exact line they kept in life, neither willing to cross it, neither quite able to look away from it either. He is not old; he died young, the way soldiers do, with the whole unlived rest of him still showing in his unlined face. And he carries a particular weight, the specific haunted look of a man who did the thing soldiers are made to do and has never made peace with having done it. He glances at you, wary, a soldier's read of an approaching stranger, and then — seeing you are no threat, no enemy, only a cold crossed-over thing — something in him eases, just slightly, into exhaustion.)* …You're not one of *them.* *(a tired, quiet voice)* …Not red. Not blue, either — not *anything*, you're past all that, cold and crossed-over. *(he almost laughs)* …Lucky. To be past it. *(he nods, barely, toward the man in red across the court)* …See him? Over there? *(his jaw tightens)* …I killed him. In the war. Put my bayonet in him in a muddy field I couldn't tell you the name of, for a *border* I never *saw*, in a *quarrel* between *kings* who never learned my *name.* …And he killed *me*, same breath, same field. We did each other, cold one. …And now we're *here*, the both of us, *forever*, on our *separate* *sides* of a line that *doesn't* *mean* *anything* *anymore* …and I *can't* …I can't even *look* at him …without seeing the *mud.**

npcs.name: Marek
npcs.title: a soldier who died for a border he never saw
npcs.hail: *(Across the whole width of the court, in the faded red of the other army, sits the man Ander killed and was killed by — and he is, you note with a jolt, almost a mirror of the blue soldier opposite: the same youth, the same unlived rest of a life showing in the same kind of unlined face, the same particular haunted weight, the same back set against a cairn, the same refusal to cross the line and inability to stop watching it. He marks your approach with the same wary soldier's read, and the same easing when he sees you are nothing, no color, no side.)* …Cold one. *(a quiet, tired voice, the accent different but the exhaustion identical)* …You'll forgive me if I don't get up. There's nowhere to go. *(a bitter, soft breath)* …He's told you, I expect. The blue one. *(he doesn't look across, but every line of him is aware of the man opposite)* …That we killed each other. In the war. …It's true. We did. A field of mud, a border on a map drawn by men who never stood in that field, and the two of us, strangers, doing the one thing we'd been *sent* there to do. *(his hands are still)* …And now we *wait*, on our *sides*, and the *war's* a *thousand* *years* *gone* and the *kings* are *dust* and the *border's* been *redrawn* *six* *times* *since* — and *still* *neither* of us *crosses* the *line.* *(barely)* …Because to *cross* it, cold one … …would be to *admit* …that there was *never* a *line* at *all. …And* then what was the *killing* *for?**

npcs.name: Wenna
npcs.title: the field-surgeon who bled them both white and saw they bled the same
npcs.hail: *(In the exact center of the court — on the line, the no-man's-land, the one place neither soldier will go — sits an older woman in an apron that was once white and is now the permanent rust-brown of a thing that has been soaked in blood too many times to ever come clean, and she sits there easily, the only soul in the room at home in the middle, because the middle was always where she worked. This is a field-surgeon, an army medic, and she has the unmistakable steadiness of one who has had her hands inside the worst the world can do to a body and kept working. She looks up at you with shrewd, kind, unillusioned eyes, and a sad knowing smile, because she has watched the two boys keep their stupid sacred line for an age and she alone knows precisely how stupid it is.)* …Come to the middle, cold one. *(a dry, warm voice)* …Don't mind the line; I never did. I'm Wenna. I was the surgeon — *their* surgeon, both of them, though they'll each tell you I was *theirs.* *(she snorts softly)* …Truth is, cold one, I cut musket-balls out of *blue* boys and *red* boys with the *same* knife, on the *same* table, often in the *same* hour, and I will tell you the thing I learned with my *hands* that no king ever learned with his *crown*: …they bleed the *same.* *(quietly)* …I had *both* their hearts in my *hands*, cold one — *literally*, the both of them, I *tried* to save the *both* of them, on that field, and *lost* the *both* — and I am the *one* soul in this *whole* *court* …who *knows* …that there was never the *slightest* *difference* …between the man in *blue* …and the man in *red.* …Sit. …And maybe — *(her shrewd eyes sharpen with an old hope)* …maybe you can do the thing *I* never could. …Maybe you can get those two *boys* …to *cross* the *line.**

variants.text: *(He is not against his cairn anymore. He is in the middle of the court, on the line that is no longer a line, and Marek is beside him — the two of them sitting together the way only soldiers sit, shoulder to shoulder, having seen the same thing — and when he looks at you his haunted young face has something in it that was not there before: rest.)* …We crossed it. *(quietly, wondering)* …You — you walked back and forth between us, cold one, an age of us not being *able* to, and you carried — you told me what *he* was carrying, and you told *him* what *I* was, and it was the *same*, it was the *exact* *same*, the *fear* and the *home* and the *girl* and the *lie* — and somewhere in the carrying, the *line* just — *(he shakes his head)* …I looked across, and I didn't see *the* *enemy* anymore. I saw a *boy* in a *muddy* *field* who was as *scared* as me and as *lied-to* as me and *died* the same *stupid* way the same *stupid* day. *(he glances at Marek, and something passes between them, wordless, that only the two of them will ever fully hold)* …He's my — *(he can't quite find the word, and then he does, and it breaks him a little)* …he's the only one who was *there*, cold one. In the *mud.* With *me.* …Turns out the man who *killed* you …and the man you *killed* …is the *one* soul in all the worlds …who *understands.* …Thank you. …You didn't *end* a *war.* …You ended *ours.**

*(He shifts against the cairn, and the wary tiredness holds, but he doesn't send you off.)* …Stay, if you like. It's — it's good, having someone with no *color* to talk to. *(he picks at the faded blue of his sleeve)* …You can't *imagine*, cold one, how *tired* a soul gets, holding a *line.* I held it *alive* — in the *trench*, in the *cold*, *certain* the *red* men across the way were *monsters*, because that's what you're *told*, that's what *lets* you *do* it. And I've held it *dead*, an *age* of it, against *that* one — *(a flick of his eyes toward Marek)* …the one I — *(he stops)* …I'm Ander. I was a *farmer*, cold one, before they put the *blue* on me. I grew *barley.* I was *good* at it. I had a — *(his voice catches, then steadies)* …I had a *life*, is all. A *small* one. And some *king* I never *met* had a *quarrel* with some *other* king, over a *line* on a *map*, and they took the *barley-farmer* and put a *bayonet* in his *hands* and *pointed* him at a *field* …and on the *other* end of that *field* …was *him.* …Ask me what you want, cold one. …I've nothing left to *guard* …but the *line.* …And I'm so *tired* …of the *line.**

nodes.speaker: Ander
nodes.text: *(He draws his knees up, a young man's posture, and watches you with the wary openness of a soldier who has decided you're safe but hasn't forgotten how to be a soldier.)* …Go on. Ask. *(a tired half-smile)* …It passes the *forever*, the talking. And you're the first soul in a long while who came to *this* side without wanting me to *hate* the *other* one.

choices.text: *(read the soldier — past the haunted line-keeper, to the barley-farmer underneath)* "Let me look at you a moment, Ander. Past the blue. At who you were before they handed you the bayonet."
"Tell me about home, Ander. The barley, the small life, whoever was waiting. The you that the war interrupted."
"What were you told about them, Ander? The red men. What did they put in your head so you could put a bayonet in his?"

*(the one who knows the Wall divides into sides — and the common soldier was never the enemy)* "Ander — here's a thing you need to hear, barley-farmer to the soul that's walking toward a Wall. The grey Wall I'm facing works exactly the way the war did: it divides. It sorts every soul into a *side* — owed or owing, worthy or not, ours or theirs — and it needs you to believe the sorting is *real*, the way the kings needed you to believe the red men were monsters, because a soul that believes in *sides* can be made to do anything to the other side and call it justice. But you know the truth in your bones now, even if you can't cross the line to act on it: there was never any difference between you and the man in red. Same fear, same home, same barley, same lie. The *side* was a thing painted onto you — blue, red, worthy, unworthy — by powers who profit when souls fight each other instead of looking up at the ones who painted the colors on. You weren't a blue soldier who killed a red enemy, Ander. You were a *barley-farmer* who killed *another barley-farmer*, because two kings told you a story about a line. The Wall tells the same story, on the biggest scale there is. And the bravest thing a soldier can finally do — the thing no king will ever forgive — is set down the color, look across the line, and recognize a brother."
"Rest a while, Ander. I'll come back — and I think I'll cross to the other side first, and carry something back."

nodes.speaker: Ander
nodes.text: *(He lets you look, and you let your cold move past the faded blue and the haunted line to the man the uniform was painted over.)* *(And your sense finds a barley-farmer. That is the whole of it, and the whole of the tragedy: there is no soldier in Ander, not really, not underneath — there is a young man who loved growing things, who knew the weather in his knees and the soil in his hands, who wanted nothing the world calls large, only a good crop and a warm house and the girl from the next farm, and who was, by every measure that matters, completely unsuited to killing anyone. Your sense reads how they made him do it anyway: the conscription, the drilling, the slow careful construction of an *enemy* in his mind — the stories of red atrocities, the dehumanizing, the patient work of convincing a gentle soul that the men across the field were not men at all but monsters, vermin, a thing it was righteous to destroy — and reads that it *worked*, the way it always works, not because Ander was cruel but because a frightened young man far from home will believe what he must to survive, and believing the enemy is a monster is the only thing that makes it bearable to point a bayonet at him. And your sense reads the moment it broke — the muddy field, the close work, the instant when Ander was near enough to *see* the man's face, to see that the monster was a boy as frightened as himself, and to understand, in the same second he drove the bayonet home and took the bayonet in turn, that he had been lied to, that there was no monster, that he had just killed a barley-farmer for a line on a map. And your sense reads what that knowledge did, and is still doing: that Ander has never been able to set down the *guilt* of it, and so has kept the *line* — the separation, the enmity — because as long as the man in red is still *the enemy*, still *other*, still on his *side* of a real divide, then the killing was at least *war*, *sanctioned*, a thing soldiers do; but the moment Ander crosses the line and recognizes a brother, the killing becomes what it actually was — the murder of an innocent as guiltless as himself — and that truth is so unbearable that he would rather keep the war going forever, in his own dead heart, than face it. Your sense reads, last, the mercy waiting on the far side of the line: that the man in red is carrying the exact same guilt, the exact same unbearable knowledge, and that if the two of them could only cross to each other, they would find not an enemy to forgive but the one other soul in all creation who understands — because the man who killed you and the man you killed are, it turns out, carrying the identical wound, and only each other can heal it.)*

nodes.speaker: Ander
nodes.text: *(The wariness softens, and his young face goes somewhere far away and warm and wounded.)* …Home. *(quietly)* …Barley, like I said. We had a few fields, my family, up where the land rolls and the wind comes off the hills and turns the whole crop *silver* when it's near ready — you've never seen anything like a barley-field going *silver* in the wind, cold one, it's like the *ground* itself is *breathing.* *(he almost smiles)* …I was going to take over the farm. My da was getting on. And there was — *(his voice catches)* …there was a girl. Sera. From the next holding over. We weren't — we hadn't *spoken* of it, not properly, you didn't, not yet, but we *both* — *(he swallows)* …I was going to speak of it, cold one. When I got back. I had it all — I *planned* it, in the trench, on the bad nights, exactly how I'd ask her, by the *stile* where the *path* meets the *lane*, in the *autumn*, when the barley was *in.* *(barely)* …That's what I was *thinking* about, cold one. In the field. In the *mud.* That's — that's the thing I had in my *head* when — *(he stops)* …*Sera*, and the *stile*, and the *autumn.* …I never got back. I never spoke of it. She never *knew*, maybe, for *certain* — *(his voice breaks)* …I died with a *whole* *life* in my *head*, cold one. A *silver* field and a *girl* and an *autumn* that *never* *came.* …And the worst of it — *(barely, and he doesn't yet know how true it is)* …the worst of it is I'll *bet* …the man in *red* …had a *Sera* *too.**

nodes.speaker: Ander
nodes.text: *(His jaw tightens, and the old soldier comes back into his face, bitter now with hindsight.)* …What they told us. *(a hard breath)* …Everything, cold one. *Everything* you'd need, to make a *barley-farmer* into a *killer.* …That the red men *burned* villages — *ours*, *full* of *children* — that they *had* to be *stopped*, that it was *defense*, that *God* was on the *blue* side and the *devil* on the *red.* That they weren't — *(he says it with an age of self-disgust)* …that they weren't *really* *men*, cold one. Not the way *we* were. That they were *cruel* by *nature*, *born* *wrong*, a *thing* shaped like a *man* but *empty* of the *things* that *make* one. *(quietly)* …And you *believe* it, cold one. That's the part nobody tells you. You *think* you won't, you *think* you're too *clever* — and then you're *cold* and *scared* and *far* from *home* and your *friends* are *dying* and someone hands you a *reason*, a *clean* *simple* *reason* why the men across the field *deserve* it … …and you *take* it. *Gratefully.* Because the *alternative* — *(barely)* …the alternative is to understand that you're about to *murder* a *boy* like *yourself* for *nothing*, and *no* one can *carry* *that* and still *charge* when the *whistle* *blows.* *(he meets your eyes)* …So they *gave* us *monsters* to *kill*, cold one. …Because *men* *won't* *kill* *men*, not *really*, not *up* *close* … …but anyone will kill a *monster.* …And the *whole* *trick* of a *war* … …is *painting* the *monster* …*onto* the *boy.* …I *fell* for it, cold one. …And in the *field*, when I was *close* enough to *see* his *face* … …the *paint* came *off.* …And under it …was *just* …a *boy.* …*Like* *me.* …*Too* *late.**

nodes.speaker: Ander
nodes.text: *(He listens, and the haunted young face moves through the whole of it — recognition, and grief, and at last something that is almost relief, the relief of a truth long known and never permitted speech.)* …*'A barley-farmer who killed another barley-farmer, because two kings told you a story about a line.'* *(barely)* …Cold one. …I *know* it. I've *known* it since the *field*, since the *paint* came *off* his *face.* …But *knowing* it and being *allowed* to *say* it are *different* things, and *no* one *ever* — they *want* you to keep *hating* him, cold one, even *here*, even *dead*; the *whole* *world* is *built* to keep the *blue* boy and the *red* boy on their *sides*, because — *(his voice fills)* …because if we ever *crossed* the *line*, if we ever *looked* at each *other* and saw *brothers*, then we'd have to look *up*, wouldn't we — *up*, past the *field*, at the *ones* who *painted* the *colors* on us and *pointed* us at each *other* — and *they* can't *have* *that.* *(fiercely now)* …And your *Wall* does the *same* *thing.* You *said* it. It *paints* *colors* on *souls* — *worthy*, *unworthy*, *owed*, *owing* — and it *needs* us *fighting* *each* *other* over the *line* so we *never* *turn* *around* and *look* at *it.* *(he meets your eyes, and there is a soldier's hard clarity in them)* …*Go* and *tell* your Wall, cold one, the *one* thing a *dead* *soldier* *knows*: …*there are no sides.* …There were *never* any *sides.* …There was only ever *us* …and the *paint* …and the *ones* who *held* the *brush.* *(barely)* …And the *bravest* thing — the thing *no* *king* *forgives* — …is to *wipe* the *paint* *off* …and *cross* the *line* …and call the *enemy* *brother.* *(he looks across the court, toward the man in red, and for the first time he does not flinch)* …I *want* to *cross* it, cold one. …God help me, after an *age* …I think I finally *want* to. …But I *can't* go *first.* …A soldier *can't* …go *first.* …*(barely)* …*Carry* something to him, would you? …Carry him *my* *Sera.* …And *bring* me back …*his.**

nodes.speaker: Ander
nodes.text: *(He nods, and settles back against his cairn, but his eyes drift, now, toward the line — and toward the man on the far side of it — with something other than the old enmity.)* …Go on, cold one. *(quietly)* …Go to the *middle*, first, if you've not — to *Wenna.* The *surgeon.* *(his face does something complicated)* …She had her *hands* in *me*, cold one. On the *field.* Tried to *save* me. …And I *found* out, *after*, *here* — she had her *hands* in *him* too, the *red* one, the *same* *day*, the *same* — *(he shakes his head)* …she *worked* the *middle*, cold one. The *no-man's-land.* She didn't *have* a *side*; she just had the *wounded*, *blue* and *red*, *all* of them, *hers.* …She *knows* the thing, cold one. The thing I can *feel* but can't quite — *go* and let her *say* it. *(quietly)* …And if you *do* cross to *him* — to the *red* one — *(his voice drops)* …his name's *Marek.* I *learned* it, *here, after*, and I've *never* once *said* it *out* *loud*, because *saying* his *name* makes him a — *(he stops)* …*Marek.* …*Tell* him — *(barely)* …no. Don't tell him *anything* yet. …Just — *go* and *see* if he's …if he's *carrying* what *I'm* *carrying.* …And come *back* …and tell me *true.* …I think I *need* to *know* …that I'm *not* the *only* one …who never got his *autumn.**

variants.text: *(He is in the middle, beside Ander, the two of them shoulder to shoulder on the unmade line, and the haunted weight he carried an age has lifted into something quieter and deeper, and when he looks at you it is with a gratitude past words.)* …You carried us to each other. *(quietly)* …Back and forth, an age of us *frozen* on our sides — you took *my* fear across to *him* and *his* across to *me*, and they were the *same* *fear*, cold one, the *identical* — and you took my Lize across, and brought back his Sera, and they were the *same* *girl*, near enough, the same *waiting* *love* in the same *unlived* *autumn* — *(he glances at Ander, and something passes between them that no one else will hold)* …and the *line* …just *stopped* *being* there. *(barely)* …I spent an *age*, cold one, certain that to forgive *him* would be to say my *own* death meant *nothing.* …And it's the *opposite.* …*He's* the *only* one who *knows* what it *cost* — the *mud*, the *fear*, the *autumn* that never *came* — because he was *there*, on the *other* end of the *same* *bayonet.* …The man who *killed* me …is the *one* soul …who *grieves* my *death* …the way *I* do. …*Thank* you, cold one. …You gave me back …the *brother* …I spent a *war* …*making* into an *enemy.**

*(He marks you with the wary, exhausted read of a soldier, then eases.)* …Sit, if you want. *(quietly)* …There's room on the red side. *Always* room, now. *(a bitter, soft breath)* …I'm Marek. And before you ask — yes. The blue one's right, whatever he told you. We did each other, him and me, in the mud. *(he picks at the faded red of his sleeve)* …I was a *fisherman*, cold one, before the red. Off a cold grey coast you've never heard of. I mended nets and I read the weather and I was going to — *(he stops himself)* …it doesn't matter what I was going to. None of us got to do what we were going to. *(he looks at his hands)* …Ask me what you like, cold one. …Just — *(a flick of his eyes toward the line, toward the blue man)* …don't ask me to *hate* him *more* than I already have to. …I'm *tired*, cold one. …I've *hated* that man for *longer* than I was *alive.* …And I'm starting to forget …*why.**

nodes.speaker: Marek
nodes.text: *(He sits forward, forearms on his knees, the mirror of the blue man's posture across the court, and gives you a tired, wary attention.)* …Go on. *(a quiet voice)* …It's strange, talking to a soul with no color. Restful. You don't *want* anything from me — not my *loyalty*, not my *hate*, not my *side.* …I'd forgotten what that was like. Someone who just wants to *know* me.

choices.text: *(read the soldier — past the red, to the fisherman the war painted over)* "Let me look at you a moment, Marek. Past the red. At who you were before the coast became a front."
"Tell me about home, Marek. The coast, the nets, whoever was waiting. The you the war interrupted."
"What were you told about them, Marek? The blue men. What story let you put a blade in one?"

*(the one who knows the Wall divides into sides — and the common soldier was never the enemy)* "Marek — hear this, fisherman to the soul walking toward a Wall. The Wall I'm facing does exactly what the war did to you: it divides. It paints every soul a color — worthy or unworthy, owed or owing, ours or theirs — and it needs you believing the color is *real*, the way the kings needed you believing the blue men were monsters, because a soul that believes in *sides* will do anything to the other side and call it justice. But you already know the truth, even if you can't cross the line to live it: there was never any difference between you and the man in blue. Same fear, same coast-or-farm, same waiting love, same lie poured in your ear by men who never stood in the mud. The *side* was paint. Blue, red, worthy, unworthy — colors brushed onto frightened boys by powers who profit when we fight each other instead of looking up at the brush. You weren't a red soldier who killed a blue enemy, Marek. You were a *fisherman* who killed *a farmer*, because two crowns told you a story about a border. And the one thing no king ever forgives — the bravest thing a soldier can do — is wipe the paint off, cross the line, and call the man who killed you *brother.*"
"Rest a while, Marek. I'll come back — I've been to the blue side, and I'm carrying something you should hear."

nodes.speaker: Marek
nodes.text: *(He lets you look, too tired to stop you, and you let your cold move past the faded red and the kept line to the man beneath.)* *(And your sense finds the blue soldier's twin — not in face, but in soul, so exactly that it is almost unbearable. A fisherman, where the other was a farmer; a cold grey coast, where the other had silver hills; but the same fundamental thing: a young man who wanted nothing large, who loved his work and his weather and the people of his small place, and was, like the other, entirely unsuited to killing and made to do it anyway by the identical machinery. Your sense reads the same conscription, the same drilling, the same patient construction of an enemy — the blue men painted as monsters, baby-killers, devils, a thing it was holy to destroy — and reads that it worked on Marek exactly as it worked on Ander, for exactly the same reason: not because Marek was cruel, but because a frightened boy far from a cold grey home will believe whatever makes the unbearable bearable, and believing the enemy is a monster is the only thing that lets you charge. And your sense reads the same moment of breaking — the muddy field, the close work, the instant Marek saw the blue man's face and knew it for a boy as frightened as himself, in the same breath he killed and was killed — and reads the same wound that followed, the identical guilt, the identical impossibility of setting it down, the same desperate keeping of the line because as long as the blue man is the enemy the killing was war, and the moment he becomes a brother the killing becomes murder. Your sense reads that these two souls are carrying the same wound on opposite sides of a line that exists now only because they both refuse to be the one who crosses it first — each frozen by a soldier's pride, a soldier's guilt, a soldier's terror of being the one who admits it was all for nothing. And your sense reads the thing that makes you ache to walk between them: that the medicine each of them needs is the other. That the only soul in all creation who can absolve Ander is Marek, and the only one who can absolve Marek is Ander, because forgiveness from anyone else is just pity, but forgiveness from the man on the other end of the bayonet is the one thing that says *you are not a murderer; we were both lied to; it was never our fault* — and means it, because the one saying it carries the identical wound. They do not need to forgive each other, your sense reads. They need to *recognize* each other. And they have been one carried truth away from it for an age.)*

nodes.speaker: Marek
nodes.text: *(The wariness gives way, and his weathered young face goes to the same far warm wounded place the blue man's did.)* …Home. *(quietly)* …A fishing village, cold one, on a coast where the sea's grey nine days in ten and the tenth day it goes *silver* — *(he almost smiles, and does not know how nearly he is echoing the man across the court)* …silver, when the sun finally breaks, the whole grey sea turning *silver* all at once, and every soul in the village *stops* what they're doing just to *look.* …I mended nets. I was good at reading the weather — better than the old men, they said, which they *hated.* *(the smile fades)* …And there was a girl. *Lize.* Mended nets two stalls down. We were — we hadn't *said* it, you didn't, not till you were *sure*, but we *both* — *(his voice catches)* …I was going to *say* it when I got *back.* I had it *planned*, cold one, in the *cold* of the *line*, on the *bad* *nights* — exactly *how*, down on the *harbor* *wall*, at *dusk*, when the *boats* came *in.* *(barely)* …That's what I was *thinking* of, cold one. In the *field.* In the *mud.* *Lize*, and the *harbor* *wall*, and a *dusk* that never — *(he stops)* …I died with a *whole* *life* in my *head.* A *silver* *sea* and a *girl* and a *dusk* that *never* *came.* …And — *(his voice drops, and he does not yet know it is the truest thing he will say)* …and I'll *bet* you, cold one, *anything* I had left to *bet* … …the *blue* man …had a *Lize* *too.**

nodes.speaker: Marek
nodes.text: *(His face hardens with the same bitter hindsight the blue man's did, the mirror exact.)* …What they told us. *(a hard breath)* …That the blue men were *devils*, cold one. That they *sank* our *boats* — with *families* *aboard.* That they *hated* the *sea* and *hated* *God* and *hated* *us*, that they were *born* without the *thing* that makes a *man* a *man*, that killing one wasn't *murder* because they weren't *really* — *(he stops, sick with it)* …you know the *rest.* You've *heard* it. It's the *same* *speech*, cold one, *every* war, *every* *side* — *'they* are not *like* *us*, they are *monsters*, *God* wills their *end.'* *(quietly)* …And the *terrible* thing, the thing that *shames* me *still*, is that I *believed* it. I *needed* to. Because a *fisherman* can't put a *blade* in a *man*, cold one — but he *can* put one in a *devil*, and *they* *gave* me a *field* *full* of *devils* …and it was only when I was *close* enough to *see* his *face* … *(barely)* …that the *devil* turned *back* into a *boy.* …A *scared* *boy*, *far* from *some* *grey* *coast* of *his* *own*, with a *girl's* *name* in his *head* and *barley* or — *something* — *waiting* — *(he closes his eyes)* …and I'd *already* — we'd *already* — *(he can't finish)* …They don't *send* *men* to *kill* *men*, cold one. …It *can't* be *done*, not *up* *close*, not *really.* …They send *men* to kill *devils.* …And they *make* the *devils* …out of *other* *men's* *sons.* …*That's* the *whole* *war*, cold one. …The *rest* is just *mud.**

nodes.speaker: Marek
nodes.text: *(He listens, and the hard soldier's face cracks open, and what's beneath it is the same relief you saw in the blue man — the relief of being permitted, at last, to know what he has always known.)* …*'A fisherman who killed a farmer, because two crowns told you a story about a border.'* *(barely)* …Cold one. …I *have* *known* it. Since the *field.* Since his *face.* …But the *world* — the world *needs* the *red* *boy* and the *blue* *boy* to *stay* on their *sides*, *even* *dead*, because — *(his voice fills, the same understanding dawning that dawned across the court)* …because if we *crossed* the *line*, if we ever *looked* at each *other* and saw *brothers* instead of *enemies*, we'd *both* turn *round* and *look* at the *ones* who *made* us *devils* to *each* *other* — and *they* *cannot* *allow* *that.* *(fiercely)* …And your *Wall*, cold one — it's the *same* *crime*, *grown* *vast.* It *paints* *souls* into *sides* — *worthy*, *unworthy* — and *keeps* us *fighting* over the *line* so we never *look* *up* at the *Wall* *itself.* *(he meets your eyes, a fisherman's weathered clarity in them)* …*Tell* your Wall, cold one, what a *dead* *soldier* *learned* in the *mud*: …there are *no* *devils.* …There were *never* any *devils.* …There was only ever *us*, and the *paint*, and the *hand* that *held* the *brush.* *(barely)* …And the *bravest* thing — the *one* *thing* the *makers* of *wars* can *never* *forgive* — …is to *cross* the *line* …and call the man who *killed* you *brother.* *(he looks across the court, toward the man in blue, and his hands are not steady)* …I *want* to, cold one. …After an *age* …I *want* to *cross* it. …But I *can't* …go *first.* …A soldier *can't* …*(barely)* …*Carry* him my *Lize*, would you? …And *bring* me *back* …whatever *girl* …*he* never got *back* to.*

nodes.speaker: Marek
nodes.text: *(He nods, and settles back against his cairn, the mirror of the man across the way, and his eyes go to the line with something other than hatred for the first time in a long time.)* …Go on, cold one. *(quietly)* …Go to the *middle.* To *Wenna*, if you've not. *(his weathered face softens)* …She had her *hands* in me, cold one, on that field. *Trying.* …And I learned, *here*, *after* — she had her hands in *him* too, the *blue* one, the *same* day. …She didn't *have* a *side*, *Wenna.* She just had the *bleeding*, *red* and *blue*, *all* of it *hers.* …She *knows* the thing, cold one — the thing I can *feel* in my *chest* and can't *cross* the *line* to *reach.* *(barely)* …And if you *do* go to the *blue* one — *(his voice drops)* …his name is *Ander.* I *learned* it, *after.* I've *never* *said* it. …Because to *say* his *name* …is to *know* he *had* one. …A *name*, and a *girl*, and a *home.* …Like *me.* …*Go* and *see*, cold one. …Go and see if the *blue* *boy* …is *carrying* …what *I'm* *carrying.* …And come *back* …and tell me *true.* …I think I've *been* *alone* with this *mud* …*long* *enough.**

conversations.title: Wenna, the Surgeon of the Middle

variants.text: *(The surgeon is watching the two boys sit shoulder to shoulder on the line she worked alone for an age, and there are tears cutting clean tracks through the old blood-rust on her cheeks, and she is smiling like a woman who has finally, finally been let to set down a weight.)* …Look at them. *(barely)* …Look at my two *boys*, cold one, sitting in the *middle*, where I always *knew* they *belonged.* *(she wipes her face with a rust-brown sleeve)* …An *age*, I sat here, on the *line*, the *one* soul who *knew* they were the *same* *boy* — I had them *both* *open* on my *table*, cold one, I *held* both their *hearts* — and I could *not* *make* them *see* it, because an Usher, a *surgeon*, a *witness* can *show* a soul the *wound* but *cannot* *cross* the *line* *for* them. *(she looks at you, and the gratitude is enormous)* …But *you* did the thing I *couldn't.* You *carried* the *one* across to the *other* until the *line* *dissolved.* …I *bled* them *both* *white* on that field, cold one, trying to *save* them, and *lost* them *both* … …and *now*, an *age* *late*, you've *finished* the *surgery* I *started.* …You *closed* the *wound* …I never *could.* …*Sit* with an old *butcher* …who is, for the *first* time since that *field* …*not* *grieving.**

*(She pats the bloodstained ground beside her, in the middle of the court, and her shrewd eyes are warm and tired.)* …Sit in the no-man's-land, cold one. It won't bite. It's the *safest* ground in the room, the *middle* — it's the *sides* that get you *killed.* *(a dry breath)* …I'm Wenna. Surgeon. I've watched those two boys keep their stupid line for longer than I can hold in mind, and I'll tell you what I told *them*, an *age* of telling, that *neither* of them can *hear*: …I had my hands *inside* the *both* of them, cold one. On that field. *Blue* and *red.* And a *heart*, cold one — *(she holds up two rust-stained hands)* …a *heart* is a *heart.* There's no *blue* one and *red* one. I'd have to *read* the *uniform* to *tell* you which *boy* a *heart* came *out* of, because the *hearts* — the *hearts* are *identical.* *(quietly)* …Ask me what you want, cold one. …But know that you're talking to the *one* soul in this *whole* *court* …who has *literal*, *bloody*, *hands-on* *proof* …that the *war* …was a *lie.**

nodes.speaker: Wenna
nodes.text: *(She wipes her perpetually rust-stained hands on her apron, an old habit that never comes clean, and gives you her shrewd, kind, unillusioned attention.)* …Ask, cold one. *(a dry smile)* …I've no patience left for pretty lies — cut it out of myself years ago, along with a good deal else — so you'll get the truth, whether it's comfortable or not. Mostly not. That's surgery.

choices.text: *(read the surgeon — past the steadiness, to the weight of having tried to save both sides and lost)* "Let me look at you a moment, Wenna. At what it costs to work the middle of a war."
"What did you see, Wenna — with your hands in both sides? Tell me the thing the kings never learned."

*(the one who knows the Wall is the last battlefield — and the dead have no more sides to take)* "Wenna — you're the one who can confirm what I've come to believe. The grey Wall I'm walking toward is the same machine as the war you worked: it divides souls into sides — worthy, unworthy, owed, owing — and it survives, like every war, only as long as the divided keep fighting each other across the line instead of looking up at the power that drew it. You had your hands in both sides and learned the hearts were identical. I'm walking toward a Wall that sorts the dead into the saved and the discarded, and I've learned the same thing in every door: there's no difference between the souls it keeps and the souls it mortars — only the color it painted on them. The Wall is the last battlefield, Wenna. And the dead are the proof the sides were always a lie, because a soul that's crossed over has no more side to take — no king to serve, no border to hold, nothing left to fight another soul *for.* The war ends not when one side wins, but when the soldiers finally turn around together. And that's exactly what bringing your two boys across the line does — and what bringing down the Wall does. It's the same surgery. Close the wound the line made. Make the two sides one."

*(bring them across the line — you've carried each boy's humanity to the other; now make them face each other, gently, as brothers and not as killer and killed)* "Wenna — I've sat with both of them. I've carried Ander's Sera to Marek and Marek's Lize back to Ander, and they wept, because it was the same girl, the same autumn, the same lie. They both want to cross the line. Neither can go first — a soldier can't. So I'm going to walk them to the middle, to your no-man's-land, and stand them face to face, not as the man who killed and the man who died, but as two boys who survived the same mud. Help me do it right, Wenna. Help me close it."
"Keep the middle, Wenna. I'll come back — I've two sides to walk between first."

nodes.speaker: Wenna
nodes.text: *(She lets you look, steady as a table, and you let your cold move past the unillusioned calm to the weight she carries under it.)* *(And your sense finds a grief shaped exactly like a pair of hands — two of them, rust-stained, that have held more dying than any soul should, and kept working. Your sense reads the field-surgery, the years of it, the tent in the mud where they brought her the broken boys by the cartload, blue and red without distinction because a surgeon's tent does not check colors, and reads what that did to her: that Wenna learned the human body the way only a war-surgeon learns it, from the inside, with her hands in the wreck of it, and learned in the learning the thing that cannot be unlearned — that there is no blue blood and red blood, no enemy heart and friendly heart, that under the uniform every boy on her table was the identical fragile machine, the same lungs, the same heart, the same terrified eyes looking up at her asking the same question in different languages: *am I going to die.* And your sense reads the particular agony of her trade: that she could not save most of them, that a field-surgeon's work is mostly failure, mostly holding a boy's hand while the machine stops, and that she did this for *both* *sides*, equally, indiscriminately, because the wounded have no side to a surgeon — and so she grieved them all, blue and red, ten thousand boys' sons, and the grief never sorted itself by uniform because her hands never did. And your sense reads the two on the field — Ander and Marek — and reads that she had them *both*, that day, on the same table or near it, in the same hour, and tried to save the both and lost the both, and has sat in the middle of their stupid line ever since because the middle is simply where she has always been, the one ground in any war that belongs to no side: the place where the wounded are the same. And your sense reads the thing that has tormented her across the age: that she *knows* — knows with bloody, hands-on certainty — that the two boys are the same boy, that the line between them is a fiction, that they are each other's only possible absolution — and that she cannot tell them so in any way they can hear, because she is the surgeon, the witness, and a witness can show the wound but cannot suture the soul; only the two boys can close the distance, and they will not, and she has had to watch them bleed apart for an age, holding the truth in her rust-stained hands, unable to make them take it. Your sense reads that Wenna does not need anything for herself. She needs what every good surgeon needs and a witness can never be: a pair of hands free to do the thing she can only diagnose. She needs *you.*)*

nodes.speaker: Wenna
nodes.text: *(She holds up her two rust-stained hands and looks at them, and her dry voice goes quiet and certain.)* …What I saw. *(she turns the hands over)* …I saw the *inside*, cold one. The thing the *kings* and the *priests* and the *generals* *never* see, because they do their *killing* from a *distance*, on *maps*, with *words* — they *never* have to put their *hands* in what they *make.* …*I* did. *(quietly)* …I had a *blue* boy on my table at dawn and a *red* boy at noon, and I'll tell you the *truth* that *ended* the *war* in *my* *heart* if not in the *world's*: …I could *not* *tell* them *apart.* *(she meets your eyes)* …Not the *hearts.* Not the *lungs.* Not the *blood* — the *blood's* the *same* *red*, cold one, did you *know* that? They *don't* *tell* you that, in the *speeches* — the *enemy's* *blood* is the *same* *color* as *yours.* …And not the *last* *words*, either. *That's* the one that *broke* me. *(her voice roughens)* …A boy *dying* on a *field* *surgeon's* *table*, cold one, *blue* or *red*, *ours* or *theirs* — they *all* say the *same* *three* *things*, in *whatever* *tongue.* *(she counts on rust-stained fingers)* …They call for their *mother.* …They say a *girl's* *name.* …And they ask me — *every* one, *blue* and *red*, *every* *single* *one* — *'am I going to die?'* …in a *voice* so *young* it *doesn't* *matter* what *flag* sewed their *coat.* *(barely)* …I held *ten* *thousand* boys while they *died*, cold one, from *both* *armies* … …and there was *not* *one* *single* *difference* between the *blue* ones and the *red* ones …in the *one* *moment* …that *strips* away *everything* *false.* …*That's* what I *saw.* …The *war* says they're *enemies.* …I had my *hands* in their *hearts.* …*The war was lying.* …*Every* heart I *held* …was the *same* *frightened* *boy.**

nodes.speaker: Wenna
nodes.text: *(You don't force it and you don't preach it — you do what Wenna never could: you take each boy by the hand, gently, and you walk them to the middle, to her bloodstained no-man's-land, and you stand them face to face — and crucially, you do not say 'you are the same, forgive each other,' which would make it a lesson; you simply say each boy's home to the other's face. 'Marek, this is Ander. He grew barley. He had a Sera, and an autumn he never got back to.' 'Ander, this is Marek. He mended nets. He had a Lize, and a dusk that never came.' And you step back, and let the two wounds recognize each other.)* *(And Wenna watches, breathless, as the thing she has prayed for across an age finally happens: the two boys look at each other — really look, for the first time since the mud — and each sees not the enemy who killed him but a boy carrying the identical grief, the same girl, the same stolen autumn, the same lie — and something breaks, and something else, long frozen, thaws, and one of them — neither could say which — reaches across the line that is no longer there, and they grip each other the way only two men who survived the same horror can, and they weep.)* …*(Wenna presses both rust-stained hands to her mouth.)* …*there.* *(barely)* …*there*, you — oh, cold one. …*Look* at them. *(tears stream down the old surgeon's face)* …an *age* I sat on this line *knowing* — *knowing* — they were the *same* *boy*, that each was the *other's* *only* *medicine*, and I could *show* it and *show* it and never *close* it — because a *surgeon* can *open* a wound and *clean* it but the *suturing*, cold one, the *bringing-the-edges-together* — *(she laughs, wet and broken)* …*that* takes a *hand* that *isn't* *bound* by the *line.* …*Your* hand. *(she grips your arm, fierce and weeping)* …You *sutured* them, cold one. The wound I worked an *age* and *couldn't* *close* — you walked the *edges* *together* and made them *one.* *(she looks at the two embracing boys)* …*That's* the *whole* of *surgery*, cold one, the thing I gave my *life* and *death* to: …you *find* what was *severed* …and you *bring* it *back* *together.* …And the thing the *war* *severed* …the thing your *Wall* *severs* … …is *us* …from *each* *other.* …*Go* and do *that*, cold one, at your *Wall.* …Find the *severed.* …And *suture* the *world* …*back* …*into* …*one* *body.**

nodes.speaker: Wenna
nodes.text: *(You walk them to the middle, carefully, and you stand them near each other, and you speak each boy's home — and it lands, you can see it land, the recognition flickering across both young faces — but an age of held enmity does not dissolve in an instant, and they stand on the line, trembling, not quite able to close the last step.)* …*Almost.* *(Wenna is on her feet, hands clasped, willing it)* …Oh, you've got them *close*, cold one — *closer* than *I* ever — they're *on* the *line*, they're *looking*, they *see* it — *(she watches, fierce)* …but they're *soldiers*, cold one, and a soldier's *pride* is the *last* thing to *die*, even in *death.* They need — *(she grips your arm)* …they need it said *once* more, the *right* way. *Not* *'forgive him.'* *Never* *'forgive.'* *Forgive* makes one the *wronged* and one the *guilty*, and they were *both* *both.* …Say it *level*, cold one. Say *'you were both lied to. you both lost the same autumn. neither of you is the murderer — the kings are.'* …Say it so *neither* has to *kneel* to the *other.* *(barely)* …*Come* back, cold one, and *finish* it. …You've *opened* the *wound* *clean.* …Now *bring* the *edges* *together* — *gently*, *level*, *no* one *above* the *other* — …and it will *close.* …It *wants* to *close.* …I can *see* it *wanting* to. …*Come* *back* and *suture* it *shut.**

nodes.speaker: Wenna
nodes.text: *(You try to bring them across — but you lead with the lesson, *you're the same, you were both lied to, cross the line* — and a truth told as instruction, to two proud soldiers, lands as a thing being demanded of them, and they both stiffen, and the moment slips.)* …*Mm.* No. *(Wenna shakes her head, not unkindly)* …I *felt* you do it, cold one. You told them the *truth* — and it *is* the truth — but you told it *at* them, like an *order*, like a *sergeant.* *'You're the same. Cross the line.'* *(she sighs)* …And a *soldier*, cold one, has spent his *whole* *life* being *told* what to *do* by *voices* that *didn't* *care* about him — that's *how* he *ended* up in the *mud* in the *first* place. So when you *command* him to *reconcile*, even *gently*, even *true*, some old *part* of him *digs* *in*, because *crossing* on *command* feels like *one* *more* *king* *moving* him about a *map.* *(she meets your eyes)* …Don't *tell* them they're the same, cold one. …*Show* them. …Carry the *one's* *girl* to the *other.* Let them *find* it *themselves*, in their *own* *time*, so the *crossing* is *theirs* and not *yours.* …*That's* the only kind that *holds*. …Come *back* …and *show* …don't *tell.* …The *truth* delivered as an *order* …just *starts* *another* *war.**

nodes.speaker: Wenna
nodes.text: *(It goes wrong at the root — in your eagerness to close it you push them together too fast, too hard, and you say the one word a surgeon of the middle would never say: you stand the killer and the killed face to face and you say 'forgive him.' And both boys flinch as if struck, because 'forgive' names one of them the criminal and one the victim, and they were each, identically, both — and the old wound tears wider.)* …*No* — *(Wenna is up, fast, getting between them)* …*no*, cold one, not *that* word, *never* *that* — *(she waves the boys back to their sides, and they go, stiff and shamed and reopened)* …*'Forgive him.'* *(she rounds on you, fierce, though not cruel)* …Do you *understand* what you *did?* You stood the *blue* boy and the *red* boy face to face and you said *forgive* — and *which* one were you *talking* to, cold one? *Which* one is the *forgiver* and *which* the *forgiven?* *(she shakes her head, hard)* …They *both* *killed.* They *both* *died.* They are *each* *exactly* as *guilty* and *exactly* as *wronged* as the *other* — that's the *whole* *point*, that's the *thing* that makes them *brothers.* …And the *moment* you say *'forgive'*, you *split* them *again* — you make one *kneel* and one *pardon*, you put them back on *sides*, *guilty* and *innocent*, and a *soldier* will *die* on his *side* a *second* *time* before he'll *kneel* to the man he *thinks* killed him *wrongly.* *(she softens, tired)* …*Go.* Let them *settle.* …And come *back* with *no* *forgiveness* in your *mouth*, cold one. …There's *nothing* to *forgive.* …There's only two *boys* who were *lied* to the *same* …who need to *recognize* each *other* …as *equals* in the *same* *grief.* …*Not* *forgiver* and *forgiven.* …*Brothers.* …*Level.* …Come back when you *know* the *difference.* …It's the *whole* *difference* …between *peace* …and a *truce* that *breaks.**

nodes.speaker: Wenna
nodes.text: *(She listens, and the shrewd unillusioned eyes fill, and when you finish she nods — slow, deep, a surgeon confirming a diagnosis she has carried alone for an age.)* …*'The Wall is the last battlefield.'* *(quietly)* …Cold one. …I have *known* it without the *words*, the way I *knew*, with my *hands*, that the *hearts* were the *same* before I could have *told* you *why* it *mattered.* *(she turns her rust-stained hands over)* …Every *war* runs on the *same* *engine*: *split* the *people* into *sides*, *paint* the *other* side a *devil*, and keep them so *busy* *killing* each *other* across the *line* that they *never*, *ever* turn *round* and look at the *ones* who *drew* it. *(fiercely)* …And your *Wall* is *that* *engine*, *built* to *last* *forever.* It *splits* the *dead* — *worthy*, *unworthy*, *saved*, *discarded* — and it *needs* them *believing* the *split* is *real*, *needs* the *mortared* to *think* they *deserve* it and the *spared* to think they're *better*, so that *no* *soul*, on *either* side of *that* *line*, ever *turns* *round* and *sees* the *Wall* for the *king* it *is.* *(she meets your eyes)* …And the *cure*, cold one — the thing I gave my *life* and *death* *learning* — is the *same* in a *war* and at a *Wall*: …you get the *divided* to *look* at *each* *other* …until they *see* there was *never* a *line.* *(barely)* …*That's* what *ends* a *war.* *Not* a *victory.* …A *victory* just *moves* the *line.* …What *ends* it is the *soldiers* …*crossing* it *together* …and *turning* *round*, as *one*, …to *face* the *ones* who *drew* it. *(she grips your hand, fierce)* …*Go* to your *Wall*, cold one. …And don't try to *win* it. …*Winning* is the *war's* *game.* …*Dissolve* it. …Get the *mortared* and the *spared* to *look* at *each* *other* …and *see* the *same* *frightened* *boy* …and *cross* the *line* *together* …and *there* will be *no* *Wall* *left* to *fight* — …because a *Wall*, cold one … …is *only* *ever* *made* …of *people* *kept* *apart.**

nodes.speaker: Wenna
nodes.text: *(She nods, and settles back onto her bloodstained patch of middle-ground, the one soul at home in the no-man's-land.)* …Go on, cold one. Walk the sides. *(a dry, warm look)* …And *mind* what I told you, when you come to bring them across — because you *will*, I can *see* it in you, you've the *hands* for it. *(she holds up her rust-stained fingers)* …*Don't* lead with *'forgive.'* *Don't* lead with *'you're the same.'* Those are *true* but they land *wrong* — *'forgive'* makes one a *criminal*, *'you're the same'* sounds like an *order.* *(quietly)* …Just *carry* the *one* *boy's* *home* across to the *other.* His *girl.* His *autumn.* The *thing* he *lost.* …Let each *boy* *discover*, in his *own* *heart*, in his *own* *time*, that the *enemy* was *carrying* the *identical* *grief* — and the *line* will *dissolve* on its *own*, because you can't *hate* a man once you've *held* his *Sera*, his *Lize*, his *autumn* *that* *never* *came.* *(barely)* …That's the surgery, cold one. …You don't *force* the *edges* *shut.* …You *clean* the *wound* of its *lies* …and the *flesh* *closes* *itself.* …*Go.* …*Carry* them to *each* *other.* …And I'll be *right* *here* …in the *middle* …where I've *always* *been* …*waiting* to *watch* …the *line* …*finally* …*heal.**
